summary: DHH celebrates Apple's WWDC announcement that iOS 15, iPadOS 15, and macOS Monterey will block email spy pixels in the Mail app, following HEY's lead. He argues that competition, not advocacy, drove this change—HEY built spy pixel blocking first, generating media attention that pressured Apple to act. He predicts the email tracking industry will be upended since even partial adoption will make open-rate data unreliable. DHH also notes the irony that Apple nearly killed HEY through App Store policies, which would have prevented the very innovation Apple is now copying.
key_points: Apple announced Mail Privacy Protection for iOS 15/macOS Monterey, blocking spy pixels and proxying requests—directly following HEY's implementation
Even modest adoption rates will destroy the reliability of email open-rate tracking, making spy pixels effectively useless
96% of US users opted out of app tracking in iOS 14.5, suggesting email spy pixel blocking will see similar uptake
Apple nearly killed HEY through App Store disputes, which would have prevented the innovation Apple is now adopting
Big platforms need small competitors to push new ideas forward—monopolistic behavior leads to stagnation
money_quotes: There's no advocacy as effective as competition.
I could have yelled and screamed about email spy pixels till I was blue in the face, but it was building a serious set of defenses into HEY that turned the argument into action.
You can't really say anything authoritatively about open rates if 5-10-30-50% of your recipients are protected against snooping.
People really do not like being tracked in everything they do online, and given the choice, will absolutely block it.
This is what the big fish like Apple never seem to be able to internalize. That they need a rich ecosystem of small fish to push new ideas forward.
If they eat or starve us all, they might get the whole pond to themselves, but it'll be an awfully stale one.
HEY's implementation may be more sophisticated, may have been earlier, but a niche player like us were never going to be able to turn the game upside down with a single shot like Apple can.
key_insight: Competition from small, opinionated products forces platform giants to adopt privacy innovations they would never pursue on their own, proving that ecosystem diversity drives progress more than advocacy alone.
